**Q1.  Why is the maximum attainable write bandwidth lower than the maximum attainable read bandwidth on MI308X and MI308HF?**

Each Compute Unit (CU) on MI300 series processors includes dedicated hardware that generates, tracks, and resolves memory access operations.  This per CU hardware for tracking memory requests handles writes and reads separately.  Treating reads and writes independently from one another enables the work associated with writing and reading memory to proceed in parallel, independently of accesses of the other type.  Some of these hardware resources have greater capacity for handling read data than they do write data.  See Figure 1 below.  This is an intentional design choice for the MI300 series processors that delivers better overall system performance than if the read and write hardware resources were perfectly balanced.  The performance improves because modern LLM (Large Language Model) kernels typically use more read data than write data.  Kernels that need more read data correspondingly experience more performance bottlenecks on read bandwidth than write bandwidth.  Because of typical LLM kernel bottlenecks and because of the asymmetric hardware read vs. write capabilities, the MI300 series processors performs better on typical LLM kernels than a symmetric design would perform.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

**_Figure 1:_**  _MI308 Block diagram with Per CU memory access resources shown_

The fact that each CU has more hardware resources capable of handling more reads than writes per CU is a design choice that involves area and power tradeoffs.  It is theoretically possible to build a system with equivalent hardware resources for handling reads and writes.  Doing so would require dedicating additional silicon area and power to support higher write-only bandwidth that is not typically used when running common LLM kernels.  Allocating additional area and power for improving per-CU write bandwidth would result in decreased area and power in other subsystems unless total area and power were increased.  Decreasing area and power in other subsystems to increase it in per-CU write bandwidth capabilities would result in lower performance on many common LLM kernels relative to the real MI300 series processors due to the common kernel behavior described above.  This theoretical system would however perform better on ‘write only maximum attainable bandwidth’ tests.

A corollary question could be ‘why is the maximum attainable bandwidth of 64 CU less than 80 CU?’  The reason is that each CU has a limited amount of hardware resources for issuing and tracking memory requests.  More CUs mean more in-flight memory transactions simultaneously which yields higher Maximum Attainable Bandwidth (MAB) all other things being equal.  Taken to an extreme, one could choose to design a system that could saturate the full memory bandwidth from a single CU.  If one built such a system using the same area and power as the MI300 series processors, it’s overall performance on LLMs would be substantially lower, because a significant amount of area and power would be wasted on hardware that would not be used in actual kernels for modern LLMs.  This extreme example illustrates the need for a balanced system design that minimizes per-CU area and power, while maximizing total system performance.  Achieving a balanced system architecture that maximizes delivered application performance was a guiding principle for the MI300 series processors, and that is why each individual CU cannot saturate the entire available system memory bandwidth.  It is also why MI300 series processors can achieve a higher maximum attainable read bandwidth vs. write bandwidth.  AMD does not expect fully optimized MAB tests to exhibit a significantly different ratio between read and write across 308X and 308HF.  AMD expects that attained bandwidth in carefully optimized LLM kernels on 64 CU MI308HF should be able to achieve similar attained bandwidth as 80 CU MI308X in most cases.

**Q2.  Why H20’s attained BW can be close to its PEAK BW, but 308 can’t (W/R 2.9/4.0TB to Peak 5.3TB).**

We’re not experts on H20 and can’t address the question of why H20’s achieved bandwidth is what it is.  What we can do is answer questions about MI308.  The portion of this question that specifically pertains to 308 is:

**Q2.A. Why isn’t MI308 maximum attainable bandwidth closer to peak bandwidth?’**

There are a multitude of factors that limit maximum attainable bandwidth relative to peak bandwidth:

- **DDR access efficiency** - Not every clock and every bit of every DDR channel can be used for transferring usable data in practice.  The peak bandwidth specification describes the maximum theoretical amount that the bus can send, but in practice many other real-world required operations get in the way of this.  An example would be DDR bank refreshes.  AMD has tuned DDR bank refreshes on MI300 series processors to be optimal for typical LLM kernels as well as many other parameters that affect overall DDR access efficiency.
- **Power** – When attempting to achieve maximum attainable bandwidth, power can become a meaningful constraint.  Increasing total system power could enable a small increase in attained bandwidth.  AMD does not recommend this because increasing power would not yield meaningful increased application performance and would decrease Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).
- **TLB misses** – TLB misses can often result in bandwidth limitations due to latency associated with page translation misses in the TLB.  The severity of this issue is dependent on the application address pattern.
- **Contention in routing memory requests** – The need to route memory accesses to many different I/O Dies (IOD) can result in contention on certain channels in the AMD Infinity Fabric.  This is dependent on the physical address access pattern used by the application and is not generally an issue in typical LLM kernels.
- **Cache bank and set contention** – Cache banking and set contention is driven entirely by address patterns used by the application.  The existing MAB tests minimize this as much as possible, but it can never achieve zero contention.

Each of these issues has been found to be a contributor to memory related performance bottlenecks in real-world LLM kernels. 

The 2.9/4.0TB bandwidth measurements on MI308 are not fully tuned results.  It may be possible to improve on those measurements with analysis and optimization of the test.  Optimizing this test to get closer to peak will not translate directly to improved model performance because LLM kernels behave differently than bandwidth-only tests.  LLM kernels mix ALU work and read bandwidth together with write bandwidth.  This real-world behavior requires individualized optimization that does not transfer directly from MAB test optimizations.
